briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for July 17, 2026. Today, we will deliver key updates on the global climate crisis, its intensifying phenomena, and the policy and civil society efforts to address it.

sections.title: 🔴 Global Climate Crisis: Intensifying Extreme Weather Events and Human Casualties
sections.content: According to reports today, climate change is leading to more rainfall, causing deadly flash floods [12], and severe floods are also occurring in West Africa due to climate change and rapid urbanization [27]. Wildfires are increasing in the United States and Canada [16], with analysis suggesting that the worst floods in Arizona are originating from wildfires, and climate change is exacerbating both [25]. In New York City, residents experienced acrid air from Canadian wildfire smoke and soaring temperatures [40], and experts warn that wildfire conditions are worsening [36]. In Europe, heatwave-related deaths were reported throughout May and June, and 'firestorms' occurred in the UK, leading to heatwave fatalities [41, 42]. These unfolding climate change disasters are leading to widespread societal issues such as flight cancellations at airports and sleep deprivation among people in India [15, 34, 38].

sections.title: ⚠️ Direct Threats of Domestic Climate Change: Spread of Migratory Pests and Lessons from Historical Droughts
sections.content: Domestically, extreme weather has accelerated the emergence of migratory pests from China, and their rapid spread in the southern coastal regions has prompted an emergency pest control alert for local governments [4, 5]. This poses a direct threat to the domestic ecosystem and agriculture. Meanwhile, historical records indicating major droughts occurring approximately every 70 years during the late Joseon Dynasty suggest the importance of long-term drought preparedness in the current climate change situation [2].

sections.title: 🌍 Changes in Climate Policy and Renewable Energy Transition Trends
sections.content: The European Union (EU) is pursuing a comprehensive overhaul of its Emissions Trading System (ETS), a key climate change policy, to alleviate the burden on industries [1]. However, some critics warn that the EU is proposing measures to slow down the pace of corporate carbon emission reductions, risking the weakening of Europe's most effective tool for greenhouse gas reduction [37, 39]. On a positive note, global renewable energy installed capacity has reached 5.2 TW (terawatts), leading to a complete restructuring of the market and changes in daily life [10, 11]. However, concerns have been raised domestically about the low probability of achieving 100 GW of renewable energy by 2030, necessitating further policy efforts [9].

sections.title: 🌱 Community-Based Carbon Neutrality Practices and Awareness Expansion
sections.content: Efforts to address climate change are actively underway in local communities as well. Eco-friendly activities based on citizen participation and carbon neutrality practices are being disseminated [6], and in Siheung City, a Climate Citizen Network will be launched on the 22nd to establish a carbon-neutral communication system with citizens [7]. Furthermore, future generations are actively participating, with high school mentors and elementary school students engaging in carbon neutrality practices together [8]. Climate change is reshaping neighborhoods in the Tampa Bay area [14], and the importance of social awareness and adaptation efforts regarding the climate crisis is being highlighted, as evidenced by surveys on Americans' beliefs and attitudes towards climate change in spring 2026 [17].

briefing.closing: As we have reviewed in today's briefing, the climate crisis is intensifying globally in various forms, and immediate responses, coupled with policy shifts and active participation from civil society, are urgently needed. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: ‘My eyes were stinging’: New Yorkers navigate smoky air and soaring temperatures
articles.description: Pollution levels in the city were elevated as smoke from Canadian wildfires drifted south across a huge swathe of the US The sun shone feebly through the thick haze. The smell of burning wood hung thick in the air. Many New Yorkers donned masks as the air quality plummeted amid health warnings.The National Weather Service issued an air-quality alert because pollution levels were elevated as smoke from raging Canadian wildfires drifted south across a huge swathe of the US, reaching all the way to

summary.phenomenon: Air pollution levels in New York City have risen as smoke from Canadian wildfires drifted south across the US, reaching New York. Consequently, New Yorkers experienced acrid air and soaring temperatures, with many wearing masks.
summary.cause: Smoke from raging Canadian wildfires drifted south across the US, extending to New York.
summary.outlook: Due to the deterioration in air quality, health warnings were issued, and New York citizens were affected, including wearing masks.
